# Preface {.unnumbered}

![](images/cover.png){width=55% fig-align="center" fig-alt="Cover of Scientometrics in R"}

Welcome to **Scientometrics in R** — a hands-on guide to bibliometrics, network science, and research evaluation using the R programming language.

## Who this book is for

This book is written for researchers, librarians, research administrators, and data scientists who want to analyze scholarly communication quantitatively. You should have a working knowledge of R (tidyverse level) and basic statistics. No prior experience with bibliometrics is assumed.

## What you will learn

By the end of this book you will be able to:

- Acquire bibliographic data from OpenAlex, Crossref, PubMed, and other sources directly from R
- Compute and interpret standard bibliometric indicators (h-index, MNCS, journal metrics) responsibly
- Build and analyze co-authorship, co-citation, and keyword co-occurrence networks
- Apply text mining and topic modeling to scientific corpora
- Create reproducible bibliometric pipelines and interactive dashboards
- Critically evaluate the limitations and ethical dimensions of quantitative research evaluation

## How this book is organized

The book is structured in seven parts plus case studies:

- **Part I** lays the conceptual and ethical foundations.
- **Part II** covers data acquisition — getting bibliographic records into R.
- **Part III** introduces core bibliometric indicators for papers, authors, journals, and institutions.
- **Part IV** builds network and mapping methods.
- **Part V** adds text and topic analysis.
- **Part VI** explores advanced topics from altmetrics to causal inference.
- **Part VII** covers production workflows — reproducible pipelines, dashboards, and Shiny apps.
- **Part VIII** presents five end-to-end case studies.

Start with the [Find Your Method](chapters/00-find-your-method.qmd) decision tree if you have a specific research question and want to jump straight to the relevant chapter.

## Reproducibility

All examples in this book run against [OpenAlex](https://openalex.org/), a free and open bibliographic database. No paid subscriptions are required to reproduce any analysis. Small sample datasets are bundled with the companion R package `scientometricsInR`.

The book is built with [Quarto](https://quarto.org/) and all code chunks execute during the build. A Docker image is provided for fully reproducible builds.

## Sibling volumes

This book is part of a series:

- [Strategy in R](https://r-heller.github.io/strategy-in-r/) — strategic management and competitive analysis
- [Methods in R](https://r-heller.github.io/methods-in-r/) — research methods and data analysis

## How to cite this book

> Heller, R. (2026). *Scientometrics in R: Bibliometrics, Network Science, and Research Evaluation*. <https://r-heller.github.io/scientometrics-in-r/>

## License

The text of this book is licensed under [CC-BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/). All code is licensed under the [MIT License](LICENSE-CODE).

## Acknowledgments

### Use of LLM tools

Portions of this book were prepared with assistance from large language
model tooling for narrowly defined, non-authorial tasks: copyediting,
prose smoothing, LaTeX/Markdown formatting, scaffolding of boilerplate
files (CI configs, CSS, build scripts), code refactoring, and citation
lookup against verified DOIs. The tools used were the latest Mistral
Le Chat model run locally via [Ollama](https://ollama.com/) and the
[`ollamar`](https://cran.r-project.org/package=ollamar) R package — all
inference performed on local hardware, with no data sent to third-party
services — and GitHub Copilot inside RStudio for code completion.

All scientific claims, methodological choices, data analyses,
interpretations, and conclusions are the author's own. No text generated
by an LLM was incorporated without review and revision by the author.
LLM tools were not used to generate, fabricate, or paraphrase cited
sources; every reference was verified against its DOI, arXiv ID, or
ISBN (see `scripts/verify_citations.R`).
